coredata.dc:title: Carrageenan-based multifunctional packaging films containing Zn-carbon dots/anthocyanin derived from Kohlrabi peel for monitoring quality and extending the shelf life of shrimps
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Ajahar
Riahi, Zohreh
Tae Kim, Jun
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Carrageenan-based active/intelligent packaging films containing anthocyanin and ZnO-doped CD (Zn-CD) from purple Kohlrabi peels were prepared for freshness monitoring and shelf-life extension of shrimp, and the influence of additives on the films' physical, functional, and structural properties was investigated. The films showed excellent UV blocking ability (85.2% of UV-A and 99.4% of UV-B) and high antioxidant effect (∼99% for ABTS and ∼ 58.6% for DPPH radical scavenging activity) and showed strong antibacterial activity to stop the growth (100%) of L. monocytogenes and to reduce the growth of E. coli by 8.1 log CFU/mL after 12 h of incubation. In shrimp packaging experiments, the films were evident in the freshness monitoring, reduced spoilage, and increased shelf life. This study suggests that next-generation biopolymer films impregnated with biomass-derived CDs and natural colorants will provide broad directions for ensuring safety and extending shelf life to meet the accelerating demand for packaging products.

head.ce:title: Carrageenan-based multifunctional packaging films containing Zn-carbon dots/anthocyanin derived from Kohlrabi peel for monitoring quality and extending the shelf life of shrimps

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carrageenan-based active/intelligent packaging films containing anthocyanin and ZnO-doped CD (Zn-CD) from purple Kohlrabi peels were prepared for freshness monitoring and shelf-life extension of shrimp, and the influence of additives on the films' physical, functional, and structural properties was investigated. The films showed excellent UV blocking ability (85.2% of UV-A and 99.4% of UV-B) and high antioxidant effect (∼99% for ABTS and ∼ 58.6% for DPPH radical scavenging activity) and showed strong antibacterial activity to stop the growth (100%) of L. monocytogenes and to reduce the growth of E. coli by 8.1 log CFU/mL after 12 h of incubation. In shrimp packaging experiments, the films were evident in the freshness monitoring, reduced spoilage, and increased shelf life. This study suggests that next-generation biopolymer films impregnated with biomass-derived CDs and natural colorants will provide broad directions for ensuring safety and extending shelf life to meet the accelerating demand for packaging products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Meat consumption continues to increase worldwide due to the demand for high-quality animal protein in the diet (Siddiqui et al., 2022). As per the baseline 2050 model projection, worldwide, the total meat consumption will reach 464 million tons by 2050 (Revell, 2015). However, raw meat products have a short shelf life regarding nutritional components, and even when stored at low temperatures, there are concerns about safety deterioration due to continuous biochemical changes and microbial activity (Bagher-Abiri et al., 2023; Cheng et al., 2022). Over time, the processed meat releases microbial metabolic products such as slime, pigments, off-odors, and discoloration associated with total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) and other volatile organic compounds (VOCs), eventually affecting its quality and leading to sensory rejection (Khan et al., 2023a). In addition, processing and transportation during the rainy season and summer can further accelerate the deterioration of meat, leading to shorter shelf life and reduced economic value (Nabi & Nourani, 2023). Therefore, research on safe, simple, economical, and environmentally friendly preservation technology for processed meat has great significance in maintaining the quality of processed meat and extending its shelf life.

Traditional packaging with petrochemical products and chemical preservatives has been demonstrated to be an effective approach for inhibiting meat decay and sensory rejection and has been widely employed in the food industry. However, the potential risk of non-biodegradable plastic and synthetic chemical residues in food products, which may cause carcinogenic risks and pose a hazard to human health, presents significant side effects (Khan et al., 2023c). Therefore, sustainable and renewable packaging materials composed of polysaccharides and other biological materials obtained from natural sources can replace petroleum-based plastics to solve the environmental problems caused by non-degradable plastic packaging materials (Khan et al., 2023d). In addition, based on the various properties of nanomaterials, packaging with desired functions can be constructed (Riahi et al., 2022a). It has been widely discussed that adding nanomaterials with natural colorants to film-forming materials can overcome the disadvantages of low barrier performance, poor mechanical properties, and low functionalities of most biopolymer films (Tammina et al., 2023).

Recently, biomass-generated carbon dots (CDs) as active fillers for biopolymer films have been introduced to meet the needs of food safety applications (Khan et al., 2023a). CDs are an innovative type of individual quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials with ultrafine diameters of less than 10 nm (Tammina et al., 2023). They possess unique properties such as photoinduced electron transfer, tunable photoluminescence, fluorescence sensing, photocatalytic processes, biocompatibility, significant chemical stability, simple functionalization, and antioxidant and antimicrobial properties (Khan et al., 2023a). Compared to other carbon nanomaterials, these materials are highly soluble in aqueous solutions and have the probability of further modification. Previous reports have tested CD as a potential crosslinker and activator in fabricating colorant-doped indicator systems for monitoring food freshness (Khan et al., 2023a). Additionally, utilizing food waste as a carbon precursor to manufacture CDs improves economic benefits by reducing environmental pollution (Khan et al., 2023a; Khan et al., 2023e).

In this study, anthocyanins and ZnO-doped CDs (Zn-CDs) from purple Kohlrabi (Brassica oleracea var. Gongylodes) peel were used to prepare carrageenan-based multifunctional films for active/intelligent food packaging purposes. Carrageenan, a linear negatively charged polysaccharide derived from red algae, is widely used to prepare wound healing hydrogels, active packaging, and freshness monitoring films (Khan et al., 2023f). In addition, carrageenan has been broadly used in food packaging materials because of its excellent film-forming ability, edible, biodegradable, solubility, and low cost (Khan et al., 2023f). On the other hand, Brassica vegetables possess anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, anti-cancer, and antioxidant properties due to various metabolites such as organic acids, phenolic compounds, minerals, flavonoids, amino acids, carbohydrates, vitamins, anthocyanins, glucosinolates, and carotenoids (Park et al., 2017). Purple Kohlrabi, one of the cruciferous vegetables, is rich in carbohydrates (polysaccharide, starch, saccharide) and protein, containing plenty of C, N, O, and H elements necessary for CD synthesis. In particular, a significant amount of anthocyanin is contained in the peel. Additionally, doping the CDs with ZnO can stabilize the final structure by fixing the surface functional groups of the CDs with metal ions (Safardoust-Hojaghan et al., 2021). Compared to synthetic colorants, anthocyanins derived from Kohlrabi are safe, palatable, and readily accessible (Kalhor et al., 2020). Previous studies have shown no side effects when anthocyanins are used as natural colorants in processed foods (Khan et al., 2023b).

Therefore, in this study, Kohlrabi anthocyanin (KA) and Zn-CD derived from Kohlrabi peel, a waste of Kohlrabi processing, were incorporated into a carrageenan matrix (Car/KA@Zn-CD) to manufacture functional food packaging films and tested their effects for monitoring quality and enhancing the preservation of shrimp. The physicochemical properties of the Car/KA@Zn-CDs films were thoroughly investigated. In addition, the impact of Zn-CD incorporation on mechanical, UV blocking, water vapor barrier, surface wettability, antibacterial and antioxidant, and pH-dependent color-shifting characteristics were evaluated.

Carrageenan (κ-carrageenan, HE-A, 1.5% aqueous solution at 75 °C with a viscosity of 50 cP) and glycerol (MW = 92.09, purity ≥ 99.0%) were supplied by Hankook Carrageenan (Jeonnam, Korea) and Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Gyeonggi-do, Korea), respectively. Zinc oxide (nanopowder, <50 nm particle size, greater than97%), ethanol (MW = 46.07, ≥95%), DPPH (hydrochloric acid, 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl, ≥99.13), ABTS (2,2-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) MW = 548.68, ≥98%), potassium persulfate (MW = 2370.32, ≥99.13), was procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Escherichia coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895, Salmonella enterica, Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 23235, and Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313 were procured from the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC, Seoul, Korea). Purple Kohlrabi and shrimp (15–18 cm, Chinese white shrimp) were obtained from a local supermarket (Seoul, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anthocyanin extraction and synthesis of Zn-CD
ce:section.ce:para: Kohlrabi anthocyanin (KA) was extracted from Kohlrabi peel using a conventional solid–liquid extraction approach (Qin et al., 2021). The Kohlrabi was washed with distilled water, peeled with a sharp knife, and dried at 40 °C in an oven. The dried and ground Kohlrabi peel powder (50 g) was soaked in 500 mL ethanol (80%) with 2 mL of HCl to stabilize KA. The extraction process was performed twice for 24 h at 4 °C to extract KA fully. The extraction solution was centrifuged and filtered to remove waste. The recovered supernatant was concentrated through a rotary evaporator.

After extracting KA, the Kohlrabi peel extract was dried at 50 °C and used as a carbon precursor to synthesize CDs. Zn-CDs were synthesized using the method of (Khan et al., 2023d). For this, 1.0 g of Kohlrabi peel powder and 50 mg of ZnO nanoparticles (5 wt% of the Kohlrabi peel powder) were dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water with gentle stirring for 10 min, and the solution mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 190 °C for 6 h (Fig. 1 ). After the reaction, the yellow–brown solution obtained by cooling to room temperature (25 °C) was filtered through a Whatman filter of 100 μm and centrifuged for 10 min at 8000 rpm. The supernatant was filtered through a Whatman membrane filter (0.22 μm pore size, 25 mm diameter nylon filter) to obtain the ZnO-doped CDs (Zn-CD) solution, and the obtained brown-yellow Zn-CD solution was stored at 4 °C prior further analysis.

Carrageenan-based smart packaging films were engineered through a solution casting method. Carrageenan (Car) (2%, w/v) was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled with stirring at 85 °C for 1 h. Glycerol (30 wt% of Car) as plasticizer was mixed into the solution with stirring for 2 h. Different concentrations of Zn-CD (1 and 3 wt%) and 10 mL of the extracted KA were added to the prepared solution mixture and continuously stirred for 2 h at 50 °C. The film solutions were cast onto a glass plate (Teflon-coated, size: 24 cm × 30 cm) and dried at room temperature for 48 h. After drying, films were peeled off the plate and stored at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity (RH) for at least 48 h. In addition, the neat carrageenan film (without KA and Zn-CD) and carrageenan film with KA were developed following a similar procedure. The prepared films were designated Car, Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CQD3 films.

Methods for physicochemical characterization of the as-prepared films and Zn-CDs are described in supporting information, including functional groups, chemical composition, mechanical properties, surface morphology, optical properties, surface color, water contact angle, and water vapor permeability.

A food spoilage test was conducted to confirm the practical applicability of the Car/KA@Zn-CD film indicator. A cut film with a circular tag was attached to the lid of a UV-sterilized container. Fresh shrimp (50 g) were placed in containers, sealed, and placed at 28 °C for 24 h for accelerated storage testing. The quantitative change in color values [L (brightness), a (redness-greenness), b (yellowness-blueness), and total color difference (ΔE)] of the indicator films were examined at every 12 h using a colorimeter (Konica Minolta CR-400 Chromameter, Tokyo, Japan) (Khan et al., 2023e).

A packaging test was conducted to determine if the Car/KA@Zn-CD film could maintain freshness, color, quality, and shelf extension during shrimp preservation. Fresh shrimp (20 g) was packed in the fabricated films (10 cm × 10 cm), sealed with a polyethylene wrap and stored at 4 °C for 15 days. Shrimp wrapped in the neat carrageenan film (Car) was used for the control test, and shrimp wrapped in the Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films were used as the experimental groups. Samples were taken every 5 days during storage, and quality analysis was repeated in triplicate. The visual aspect of the packaged product was checked by checking the color quality and measuring the pH value. The total viable colony count (TVC) approach was employed to determine the number of bacteria in each group's packaged samples. Test samples (0.1% of 45 mL peptone water) were homogenized for 180 s using a stomacher (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France). Then, 3 M™ Petrifilm counting plates with 1 mL serially diluted homogenous aliquots were replicated. Colonies were counted after incubation for 48 h at 37 °C, and the data were depicted as log CFU/mL.

All experiments were repeated in triplicate, and data are depicted as mean ± SD (standard deviation). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was executed, and Duncan's multiple range test was used for significant difference (p less than 0.05) analysis of each mean value using SPSS statistical analysis software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The microstructure and morphology of the as-prepared Zn-CD recorded by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are illustrated in Fig. 2 a. The Zn-CDs were monodispersed in various shapes with less than 10 nm diameter (in the quantum range). Fig. 2b shows Zn-CDs’ particle size distribution positioned at different locations by fitting the histograms to a Gaussian model. The particle diameters of Zn-CDs ranged from 5.16 to 8.91 nm, and the average diameter of CDs was 8.5 ± 0.2 nm.

The Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the synthesized Zn-CDs is depicted in Fig. 2c. The peaks at 3200 and 3080 cm−1 are due to the –OH and NH bonds stretching vibrations (Khan et al., 2023c). The symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of CH and CH2 bonds in methyl and methylene groups can be assigned by peaks at ∼ 2926 and ∼ 1238 cm−1. The absorption peaks at ∼ 1740, ∼1397, and 1057 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibration of CO, CN, and COC bonds, respectively (Fu et al., 2022). Further, a peak was noticed in the region of 997 to 832 cm−1, ascribed to stretching vibrations of the Zn-O bond (Valerio et al., 2019). The sharp and intense absorption peaks for carboxyl and hydroxyl groups and the amide and Zn-O moieties in the FTIR spectrum verified the hydrophilic nature of Zn-CD with abundant surface functional groups. It can be depicted from Fig. 2c that the incorporation of ZnO can react with surface CO groups of the CD, which leads to the reduction of CO content (Xu et al., 2016). It is well known that CO groups are vital passivation resources for CDs; therefore, diminishing the characteristic peak of CO confirms the interaction between metal and CD’ surface. Further, the broadening of the peak around 3200–3500 cm−1 with the slight shifts in the peak position of the participating groups in FTIR spectra of Zn-CD infers that intermolecular hydrogen bonding between OH groups of CD and ZnO also plays a role in Zn-CD formation (Chen et al., 2021).

Additionally, the optical characteristics of Zn-CDs were investigated. As displayed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2d, black line), there was a strong absorption band around 300 nm and a lump near 280 nm, which could be assigned to π → π* transitions of the aromatic structure containing CO, and CN, respectively (Khan et al., 2023d). The emission of blue fluorescence by Zn-CD solution under exposure to 365 nm UV light (inset, Fig. 2d) confirms CD synthesis. Fluorescence emission intensity under UV light is a characteristic of CDs defined by the carbon source and synthesis method (Khan et al., 2023d).

As shown in Fig. 2d, the Zn-CDs exhibited maximum fluorescence (FL) emission at 442 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm. As observed in the FL spectra, Zn-CDs exhibited a typical excitation wavelength-dependent phenomenon in which the FL emission peak red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 2e). A previous report investigated the tunable FL emission properties of CDs related to various surface defects and nonuniform size of nanoparticles (Khan et al., 2023f).

The most common route to estimate the ability of substances to neutralize or scavenge free radicals is to analyze their antioxidant activity. The antioxidant potential of Zn-CD was estimated using ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging methods. Antioxidation experiments were performed by adding various Zn-CD concentrations to aqueous solutions of ABTS and DPPH dissolved in methanol. After 30 min of incubation, absorbance decreases were recorded at 517 and 734 nm for DPPH and ABTS, respectively. The radical scavenging activity of Zn-CDs against ABTS and DPPH enhanced in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 2f). At the concentration of 12.5 μg/mL, the antioxidant activity of Zn-CDs reached 25.1 ± 2.1% for ABTS and around 18.8 ± 1.9% for DPPH. As shown in Fig. 2f, the antioxidant efficacy for both radicals was enhanced with increasing concentration of Zn-CD, and ABTS scavenging activity (99.65 ± 2.8%) was higher than DPPH scavenging activity (93.1 ± 2.3%) at 100 μg/mL concentration. The higher antioxidant activity for ABTS was ascribed to the hydrophilic nature of Zn-CDs, which made CD more easily solubility in ABTS (aqueous) than in methanol DPPH solution (Khan et al., 2023d; Riahi et al., 2022b). The higher the number of carboxyls and –OH groups on the Zn-CD surface, the stronger the radical scavenging action. The scavenging effect of ABTS• and DPPH• can be lowered and quenched by H• transfer from –OH of Zn-CDs to DPPH-H and ABTS-H (Murru et al., 2020).

The antibacterial activities of the Zn-CDs against S. enterica, E. coli, S. aureus, and L. monocytogenes determined through the agar well-diffusion assay and MIC/ MBC assessment were presented in Fig. 2g and Table S1 (supporting information). The MIC/MBC values of Zn-CDs for S. enterica and E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) were 200/300 and 600/700 μg/mL, while those for S. aureus and L. monocytogenes (Gram-positive bacteria) were 200/300 and 300/400 μg/mL, respectively (Table S1). The inhibition zone ranged from ∼ 11.8 mm and ∼ 7.8 mm for S. aureus and L. monocytogenes and ∼ 10.3 mm and ∼ 5.2 mm for S. enterica and E. coli, respectively. The results suggest that the Zn-CDs can effectively inhibit the growth of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. However, the efficacy of the Zn-CDs was found to be more effective against Gram-positive than Gram-negative bacteria. The reason may be that Gram-positive bacteria's single peptidoglycan cell wall layer promotes strong Zn-CD interactions with peptide sequences readily ligated into the cell interior. The thick bilayer peptidoglycan cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria makes interaction with Zn-CD more difficult (Khan et al., 2023d; Riahi et al., 2023). Regarding the antibacterial action of the CDs, the previous investigations report that CDs get bound to the surface of the bacterial cells and thereby inducing reactive oxygen species (ROS) by transferring oxygen molecules, which create hydroxyl free radicals (OH•) and singlet molecular oxygen (1O2) formation, affecting the antioxidant and oxidative balance which in turn destroy the biomolecules found in the cell wall leading to the lysis of the cell (Khan et al., 2023f). Another cause of antimicrobial action could be the release of Zn2+ ions from Zn-CDs, which interact with the cytoplasmic material of the bacterial cell to produce ROS, which can subsequently break down the bacterial cell wall and induce cell lysis (Tammina et al., 2020).

FTIR spectra were assessed to determine the fabricated films' surface functional groups. The FTIR spectra of the Car, Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1 and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films are shown in Fig. 3 a. The broad absorption peaks around 3200–3500 cm−1 appearing in all of the films have corresponded to vibrations of water (–OH) absorbed on the surface of the films (Khan et al., 2023f). After adding KA and Zn-CD, the peak around 3200 cm−1 broadened due to the synergistic stretching of hydrogen-bonded NH groups. The OH and NH stretching of the amide group coupled with hydrogen can be responsible for this broad peak (Khan et al., 2023d). The peaks at ∼ 2925, 2857 and ∼ 1372 cm−1 were indicated to the CH, CH2 and CN modes of vibration, respectively, whereas the peaks at 1732, 1644, and 692 cm−1 could be attributed to the CO, CC and CS groups, respectively (Zhao et al., 2015). The aromatic ring stretching peaks (1480–1390 cm−1 and 1700–1500 cm−1), corresponding to anthocyanin, are consistent with their existence in the Car/KA@Zn-CD films. The peaks below 1000 cm−1 can be associated with Zn-O bond vibrations (997 to 832 cm−1) in the Zn-CDs (Valerio et al., 2019). Slight shifting in the positions of the peaks at 3200–3500 cm−1 and 1700–1400 cm−1 in the neat Car and Car/KA@Zn-CD films indicate that the OH groups in the anthocyanins made hydrogen bonds with the NH and carboxyl groups of Zn-CDs and Car (Ezati & Rhim, 2020). The reason for the analogous FTIR spectra for all films might be the overlapping of –OH, CH, CC, NH, and CS stretching in the similar IR region.

Similarly, the XRD diffractograms were recorded to depict the prepared films' crystalline morphology and phase recognition. Fig. 3b shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) diffractograms of the Car, Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films. All films show broad diffraction peaks at 2θ ∼ 20°, indicating high crystallinity due to the amorphous nature of Car, steady with previously reported XRD patterns of biopolymer films (Ali et al., 2023). Similar broad diffraction peaks were observed when the Zn-CDs were added, indicating an amorphous biopolymer matrix like the neat Car and Car/KA films. However, additional diffraction peaks around 30.8, 36.1, 47.5, and 57.1° correspond to (100), (101), (102), and (110) plane of the ZnO (PDF 36–1451), indicating the Zn-CDs’ presence within the composite films (Zhang et al., 2015).

Additionally, the information for chemical composition, elemental status and bond structure within the constituent components of Car/KA@Zn-CD films were investigated by analyzing XPS spectra. The full survey and deconvoluted X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were employed to analyze the chemical composition and surface properties of the neat Car and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films. As shown in Fig. 3c, the neat Car and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films comprised C 1 s, S 2p, and O 1 s peaks at binding energies of approximately 283.1, 168.1, and 530.1 eV, respectively. However, the additional N 1 s and Zn 2p peaks around ∼ 398.1 and ∼ 1021.1 eV, respectively, verified the successful incorporation of the Zn-CD in the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 composite (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 3d, the main peak in the high-resolution C 1 s spectra of the Car and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films could be split into four peaks. The CC/CH bond with the sp2 orbital indicates the peak at 284.1 eV. Further, the 285.6, 287.0, and 288.1 eV peaks correspond to CN/CC, CS/CO, and OCO or CO (carbonyl) bonding structure, respectively. The high-resolution S 2p spectra of both films (Fig. 3e) were divided into three peaks of the CSO, -OSO3 2− (S 2p3/2), and -OSO3 2− (S 2p1/2) groups at binding energies of ∼ 167.3 eV, ∼ 168.4 eV, and ∼ 169.2 eV, respectively (Wang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021). For O 1 s deconvoluted form Zn-O/CO, COH/COC, and OCO bonds can be indicated by the three component peaks appeared around ∼ 530.2 eV, 531.4 eV, and 532.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 3f) (Zhang et al., 2015). For N 1 s (Fig. 3g), the CNC, N-(C)3, and NH bonds can be assigned by the peaks at 397.9, 398.9, and 399.5 eV, respectively (Khan et al., 2023f). Also, Fig. 3h shows two peaks fitted at 1021.1 and 1044.5 eV correspond to the Zn 2p 3/2 and Zn 2p 1/2 of ZnO, respectively, indicating the Zn2+ state of the Zn-CD (He et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2015).

FE-SEM spectroscopy is crucial for the assessment of the microstructure and morphological characteristics of the packaging films. Fig. S1a (supporting information) depicts the surface and cross-section view of the Car, Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films investigated through field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The surface of the pristine-Car film was compact and smooth, free from voids and cracks. When KA was added, the films' surface remained smooth, signifying that KA and Car are compatibly blended to make homogenously mixed Car/KA films. However, after adding Zn-CD to the composite film, the surface was slightly warped, and when 3 wt% of Zn-CD was incorporated, the film surface showed fine swelling and wavy lumps due to the hydrophilic interaction of Zn-CD. However, the homogeneity and smoothness of the film remain unchanged (Fig. S1a). The cross-section of the neat Car, Car/KA films showed some folds and surface cracks, and with the addition of 3 wt% of Zn-CDs, the cross-section of the film gradually became smooth and more compact, indicating that the CD improved the internal structure of composite films. It is believed that the formation of hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions between the Car surface groups and the hydroxyl groups of anthocyanins and Zn-CDs contributed to improving the compactness of the composite films (Zhou et al., 2021).

The elemental composition on the surface of the fabricated film was evaluated by the EDS technique. The EDS images of the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film are shown in Fig. S2. C, N, S, O, and Zn elements were unvaryingly distributed in the film matrix. A small amount of Zn element and N element confirms the presence of Zn-CDs in the composite structure. It is worth noting that the Zn and N elements are evenly distributed on the film surface, further verifying that the Zn-CDs are evenly dispersed in the film matrix.

The active films' visual appearance can be changed by incorporating functional nanomaterials into the biopolymer matrix and are vital as the film’s transparency secures visual evidence and satisfy the consumers. Therefore, assessing the optical and color properties of the developed films is vital. Fig. S1b shows the actual hue of the fabricated films, whereas the quantitative assessment of color changes, i.e., L, a, and b values along with total color difference (ΔE), are listed in Table S2 (supporting information). The neat Car film possessed an l -value (lightness) of 91.1, making it very transparent. However, the addition of KA and KA/Zn-CD significantly affected the brightness of the composite films. The films tended to exhibit a slightly reddish-brown tint with a greater decrease in brightness with increasing Zn-CD concentration. The a-value (redness) of the composite film changed significantly from 4.2 (neat Car) to 40.1 (Car/KA) due to the red color of anthocyanin. However, for the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film, the redness further intensified (a = 47.4) due to the synergetic color combination of red anthocyanin and yellowish-brown Zn-CD. Also, the addition of anthocyanin and Zn-CD significantly increased the yellowness (b-value) of the film. As a result, the total color difference (ΔE) increased significantly from 6.4 (neat Car) to 54.8 (Car/KA@Zn-CD3) with the addition of anthocyanin and Zn-CD.

Packaging films comprising UV shielding characteristics can protect the quality of packaged products from photochemical oxidation. UV radiation exposure is responsible for deleting processed food's color, flavor, odor, and nutritional value. Therefore, the UV–vis light barrier characteristics of the carrageenan-based films were examined by recording light transmittance spectra in the 200–800 nm (Fig. S1c,d). Fig. S1c and Table S2 show that the neat Car film was highly transparent with 90.2% light transmittance at 660 nm (T660). The small shoulder peak in the UV region at ∼ 280 nm demonstrates the characteristics phenomenon of biopolymer arising due to the π-π* transition of the carboxylic group (CO). Films containing KA and Zn-CD showed a slight decrease in light transmittance (T660), while the UV blocking abilities (T280) of the films were significantly enhanced (Table S2 ). The appearance of the broad and sharp transmittance peak around ∼ 550 nm of Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films was due to anthocyanin's light absorption at ∼ 550 nm (Fig. S1c). In addition, an increase in the UV blocking ability (∼85.2 ± 1.8% of UV-A and 99.4 ± 1.9% of UV-B) was observed as the Zn-CD concentration increased to 3 wt% (Fig. S1d). This is because anthocyanins and Zn-CDs contain significant amounts of polyphenolic compounds rich in aromatic groups and strong UV–vis light absorbers (Khan et al., 2023e; Roy & Rhim, 2021). As a result, the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film can block UV light effectively, assuring food stability and extending the product shelf life of high-fat foods.

The mechanical properties of fabricated films in terms of tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and elastic modulus (EM) were analyzed, and the results are listed in Table S3. As depicted in Table S3, the thickness of the composite film substantially increased from 54.2 ± 1.5 μm (for neat Car) to 65.1 ± 1.8 μm (Car/KA@Zn-CD3) with the addition of KA and Zn-CD due to the increase in the solid content. However, the addition of functional fillers had not much impact on the mechanical characteristics of the Car film. The TS (52.6 MPa), EB (4.2%), and EM (stiffness, 1.9 GPa) data for neat-Car suggested the stiff, firm, and strong nature of the biopolymer film. With the incorporation of KA, the TS, and EM of the Car/KA films decreased, whereas the EB was increased by 95.2% because anthocyanins act as a plasticizer that improves the molecular mobility of the biopolymer molecules within the film matrix, thereby increasing its flexibility (Zhai et al., 2017). However, incorporating 3 wt% of Zn-CD improved the composite film's TS (51.1 MPa) compared to the film blended with KA.

In contrast, adding Zn-CDs increased their flexibility with increasing EM (stiffness) to 2.0 GPA. In particular, the tensile strength of the Car film wasn't reduced with the addition of Zn-CDs and KA, which is important in contrast to earlier reports that the mechanical characteristics of many biopolymer-based films generally decreased when bioactive natural fillers were introduced (Hajinezhad et al., 2020). The result of the Car film’s mechanical properties not changing significantly by adding hydrophilic Zn-CDs and KA was possibly due to the intermolecular interactions between the polymer matrix and the nanofillers (Roy & Rhim, 2021; Zhai et al., 2017).

The packaging films attributing suitable antioxidant agents can neutralize or scavenges unwanted compounds such as radical oxidative species released during food and help extend the packaged products' shelf life (Khan et al., 2023c). Therefore, the antioxidant efficacy of the carrageenan-based films was assessed by defining their capacity to scavenge DPPH and ABTS radicals, and the results are shown in Fig. S3a (supporting information). The antioxidant efficacy of the neat Car film was negligible. However, the KA-added film showed free radical scavenging activity of 31.2 ± 1.7% in ABTS and 14.2 ± 1.2% in DPPH. In contrast, Hao et al. (2023) reported that adding black rice bran anthocyanins did not increase the antioxidant activity of chitosan-based films.

On the other hand, the addition of Zn-CDs significantly (p less than 0.05) improved the antioxidant activity of the films, and the films containing 3 wt% Zn-CDs (Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film) showed 99.1 ± 1.8% of ABTS•+ and 58.6 ± 1.5% of DPPH• radical scavenging action. The enhanced antioxidant efficacy of the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film is believed to be due to the active functional moieties (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) of Zn-CDs and the presence of numerous phenolic groups in anthocyanins that scavenge free radicals and block free radical chain reactions (Etxabide et al., 2021; Khan et al., 2023d). These findings confirmed that the composite film containing KA and Zn-CD has pH responsiveness and excellent antioxidant activity, making it suitable for the active packaging of foods with high lipid content.

The packaging films comprising antimicrobial agents can be promising to delay microbial expansion by decreasing the live counts of bacterial strains by extending the lag period and helping to preserve the packaged food for a longer time (Khan et al., 2023c). The antibacterial potential of the carrageenan-based films was evaluated against foodborne bacterial strains such as E. coli and L. monocytogenes through the viable colony count approach, and the results are depicted in Fig. S3b,c (supporting information). The neat Car film did not show antibacterial activity against both bacterial strains, indicating that the polysaccharide does not have an antimicrobial function (Khan et al., 2023f). However, the Car/KA film showed weak antibacterial activity mainly due to the phenolic group of anthocyanin, which interacts with the bacterial cell wall and increases cell permeability (Roy & Rhim, 2021). On the other hand, the addition of Zn-CDs greatly enhanced the antibacterial activity of the film, and the antibacterial activity increased with increasing CD concentration. The film containing 3 wt% of Zn-CDs (Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film) inhibited the growth of E. coli by 8.1 ± 0.0 log CFU/mL than the control after incubation of 12 h, whereas the growth of L. monocytogenes was completely stopped after 9 h of exposure. The antibacterial effect of the film is in good agreement with that of Zn-CDs (Fig. 2g). It is well-known that CD-added biopolymer films have high antibacterial properties (Khan et al., 2023a). The high antibacterial potential of Car/KA@Zn-CD films may be because of the synergistic effect of anthocyanins and Zn-CD. The presence of phenolic groups on the surface of anthocyanin and the release of Zn2+ ions, hydroxyl free radicals (OH•) and singlet molecular oxygen (1O2) by the Zn-CD, bound with the biomolecules found in the bacterial cell wall stimulates the intracellular components’ leakage, resulting in cell death (Roy & Rhim, 2021; Tammina et al., 2020).

A food's pH frequently changes with its quality, freshness, and safety change during storage. As a result, measures of pH variation can be used to track changes in these essential quality aspects of packaged food (Khan et al., 2023c). Therefore, the pH indicator ability of films containing KA and KA@Zn-CD as functional fillers was analyzed using buffers of different pH (Fig. 4 a). The color change of the KA solution and the KA@ZnCD film showed similar patterns. The color change of KA due to the structural transition of anthocyanins appeared red color (flavylium cation) in acidic pH (2–6), whereas the color of the solution shifted from purple/blue (carbinol pseudo-base) in neutral pH to green at pH 12 (chalcone form) (Roy et al., 2021). Similarly, adding KA and Zn-CD composite films provided a sensing ability with predictable color change with pH changes due to the molecular structural transitions of the anthocyanin (Fig. 4b).

The films' color values (L, a, and b) and total color difference (ΔE) in different buffer solutions are shown in Fig. 4c,d. Due to the pH responsiveness of KA, the L-value decreased for all films as the pH of the buffer solution increased from 2 to 10. In all films, the a-value (redness) decreased rapidly as the pH increased from 2.0 to 6.0, and the red color (due to flavylium cations) gradually became lighter, which was further confirmed by decreasing the b-value (yellowness). In addition, when the pH was 7 to 12, the a-value was recorded as a negative number, and the b-value showed a gentle increase as the film's color changed from purple to green. In addition, the ΔE of the film changed significantly as the pH value changed from acidic to alkaline. According to the literature, other anthocyanin-rich biopolymer films exhibited similar pH sensitivities (Roy et al., 2021).

The freshness of perishable protein-rich meals like shrimp and meat can be monitored using colorimetric smart films that react to the formation of volatile chemicals like trimethylamine and ammonia that are regularly generated during meat product degradation (Khan et al., 2023c). The ammonia and acetic acid sensitivity of the carrageenan-based smart films are displayed in Fig. 5 a. The clean Car film did not shift color when treated to ammonia and acid vapors because the polysaccharides do not contain coloring pigments that react to these gases. In contrast, the Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films showed a distinct color change. For the films doped with KA and Zn-CD, the bright red color gradually changed to green within 15 min when the film was exposed to ammonia vapor. Ammonia gas reacts with moisture to form hydroxyl ions, creating an alkaline environment around the film. Therefore, when exposed to ammonia vapor, anthocyanins are transformed into the chalcone form, giving them a green color. However, in an acetic acid atmosphere, the red color of the film is maintained without significant change due to the flavylium cation of anthocyanin at acidic pH.

The quantitative color values (L, a, b, and ΔE) recorded during the 15 min of exposure to acid and ammonia are shown in Fig. 5b,c. For the Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1 and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films, a sharp decrease in the a-value and a significant increase in the b-value of the film on ammonia exposure verified the color shift from red to green. The total color difference (ΔE) and change in the a- and b-values of the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film were more significant, confirming more intense color change than the Car/KA and Car/KA@Zn-CD1 films.

Shrimp are highly prone to microbial infections and produce TVB-N during storage, causing pH and color changes in intelligent packaging (Khan et al., 2023e). The changing color of the Car/KA@Zn-CD film tag is shown in Fig. 5d, and the TVB-N and pH values are shown in Table S4 (supporting information). Visual and quantitative color changes of indicator films placed inside shrimp packages stored at 28 °C for 24 h were recorded every 12 h, and the neat Car film was used as a control for comparison. On the first day, the color of the Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films appeared red (Fig. 5d), but the pH and the TVB-N values for the shrimp were 5.9 ± 0.3 and 8.2 ± 1.4 mg/g, respectively (Table S4). The film's red color and low pH and TVB-N values confirmed that the packaged food was fresh and suitable for consumption. However, over time, the initial red tint of the Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films gradually turned green (Fig. 5d), while the pH and TVB-N values rose to 7.8 ± 0.3 and 26.3 ± 2.8 mg/l00 g, respectively as the shrimp began to degrade during storage (Table S4). The green color of the film indicates that the shrimp has spoiled and is no longer suitable for human consumption. The results are consistent with those of Liu et al. (2018), who observed that the pH and TVB-N values for packaged shrimp were increased to more than 7 and 25 mg/100 g after 24 h storage at 25 °C due to microbial contamination.

In addition, as shown in Fig. 5e, after 24 h of storage, the a-value of the films rapidly decreased, while the b-value slightly decreased as the TVB-N and pH values rose during shrimp spoilage, and the films turned green. The a-values decreased from 47.42 to −11.7, and the b-values decreased from 1.5 to −1.43, with the maximum for Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films. These color values quantitatively confirmed that the initial red-to-green transition was more intense for the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film than for the other films due to the synergistic combination of red color anthocyanin and yellowish brown Zn-CD, which further intensified the final color and made the composite film more prone to visual detection. In addition, the total color difference (ΔE) of the film during storage was greater than 5, indicating that even unskilled panelists could recognize the color change (Fig. 5f) (Han et al., 2017).

The efficacy of the fabricated films was evaluated through shrimp packaging. Shrimp were wrapped in control (neat Car), Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films preserved at 4 °C for 15 days and investigated the quality change. As shown in Fig. 6 a, The shrimp wrapped in the control and Car/KA films turned from gray to orange within 5 days, and the Car/KA film turned green, indicating that the shrimp was unsuitable for consumption. However, shrimp wrapped with Car/KA@Zn-CD1 and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films showed no color change, and the color of the film remained the same even after 5 days of storage, confirming the shrimp quality can be preserved in terms of shape and color. In particular, the quality of shrimp using the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film was visually unchanged, with no sign of food decomposition. The color of the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film did not change even for up to 10 days of storage, ensuring the excellent quality and safety of the shrimp. Since then, food quality has started declining (Fig. 6a).

In addition, changes in pH, redness (a-value), and TVC (total viable count) values of packaged shrimp during storage were investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 6b-d. The pH of shrimps packaged with the neat Car, Car/KA, Car/KA@Zn-CD1, and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films gradually increased in the range of 5.9–8.6, 5.9–8.1, 5.9–7.7, and 5.9–7.4 respectively (Fig. 6b). The accumulation of nitrogen molecules produced by microbial growth is the main cause of the pH rise, and a higher pH value (above 7) is evidence of the onset of shrimp decay. Shrimp wrapped with Zn-CD-added films was significantly better preserved than shrimp wrapped with the neat Car and Car/KA films. For example, the pH value of shrimps wrapped with Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film was much lower than that of shrimps wrapped with other films after 15 days.

Also, the change of the shrimp from gray to red/yellow confirms the shrimp's deterioration. Therefore, the color difference of these packaged shrimp samples was quantitatively confirmed by measuring the a-value of the shrimp. As shown in Fig. 6c, the a-value of the packaged shrimp differed depending on the film type. On the 15th day of storage, the a-value of the shrimp wrapped with the neat Car film increased the most from 1.7 ± 0.2 to 16.9 ± 0.3. However, shrimp packaged with the Car/KA@Zn-CD1 and Car/KA@Zn-CD3 films significantly increased a-values during storage, from 1.5 ± 0.3 to 7.9 ± 0.2 and from 1.5 ± 0.3 to 7.1 ± 0.4, respectively. The color value of shrimp, especially the a-value results, demonstrated the effectiveness of Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film in shrimp preservation.

The degree of microorganism proliferation is an important indicator for determining the quality of marine products. The TVC of each film-wrapped shrimp increased steadily over the storage period, as shown in Fig. 6d. The TVC of shrimp wrapped with the neat Car film increased from 2.7 ± 0.3 log CFU/mL on day 0 to 9.4 ± 0.3 log CFU/mL on day 15. However, shrimp wrapped with the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film showed fewer TVCs. Compared to the Car, Car/KA, and Car/KA@Zn-CD1 films, it was found to be near the maximum allowable limit of shrimp quality (7 log CFU/mL) (Khan et al., 2023e). These results indicate that the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film can effectively prevent the growth of foodborne pathogenic bacteria and extend the shelf life of shrimp.

The overall results suggest that the Car/KA@Zn-CD3 film had greater UV-blocking, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activity than the other films, likely due to the synergistic effects of KA and high concentration of Zn-CD, and thus extends the shelf life of shrimp.

Anthocyanin (KA) is extracted from Kohlrabi skins, a by-product of agricultural food processing, and Zn-doped carbon dots (Zn-CDs) were prepared using the extracted residue, and the KA and Zn-CDs were used to prepare carrageenan-based pH-responsive, active and intelligent food packaging films. The KA and Zn-CDs were compatible with the carrageenan matrix to make non-covalent interactions (hydrogen bonding). Zn-CDs provided excellent antioxidant, antibacterial, and UV-blocking capabilities that imparted functionality to the manufactured film without affecting mechanical properties, while KA imparted pH-dependent color-changing properties to the film. Furthermore, active packaging experiments of shrimp demonstrated that the Car/KA@Zn-CD films extended the shelf life and maintained the quality of the packaged shrimp. The Car/KA@Zn-CD films are expected to realize high-value utilization of biomass-derived fillers to meet the demand for sustainable smart packaging. In addition, based on the experimental results, it can be suggested that using CD and natural pigments as functional fillers is an innovative tool for the future development of active and intelligent food packaging.
